hypothesis: When price hits the extreme ends of the Williams %R, volume momentum measured by OBV tends to confirm an overextended move; mean‑reversion is expected as the price pulls back toward the middle band, while ATR‑based stops protect against rare continuation.

Bollinger band extremes capture price overextension in a range-bound market; a complementary RSI filter confirms weak momentum, while ATR-based risk limits ensure only sufficiently volatile periods are traded, reducing false entries and improving risk-adjusted performance.

When price hits an extreme on Williams %R, OBV momentum indicates whether buying or selling pressure will pull the price back toward the middle, so a mean‑reversion entry on the extreme combined with an OBV trend filter reduces false reversals; ATR provides a volatility‑adjusted risk envelope.

When the price reaches the extreme lower band of Williams %R while OBV shows an upward trend, it signals a short-term overbought pressure that is likely to reverse, and vice versa for the upper band; using ATR to set dynamic stops and targets protects against volatility spikes while maintaining a 2.4:1 risk/reward ratio.

Bollinger bands provide price levels that the market tends to return to after extreme moves; OBV confirms that the volume flow is supporting a reversal rather than a continuation, reducing false breakouts; ATR‑based risk limits adapt to current volatility so that stop‑losses and take‑profits are appropriately sized for the asset’s 30‑minute swings.
